description: Maps Apple DFU/restore error codes and Apple Diagnostics reference codes to board-level diagnostic tree steps. Use this to jump directly to the relevant section when a specific error code is known.
usage: When a device reports an error code, look it up here to find which diagnostic tree step(s) to start at instead of following the full sequential flow. This saves time by skipping irrelevant subsystems.
sources: The iPhone Wiki / The Apple Wiki restore error databases
Rossmann Group forums (A2179 error 4013, A2442 error 4041 case studies)
LogiWiki DFU Mode Restore and Apple Diagnostics references
Cross-referenced with Eason (iphone_cpu_fix) M1 standby circuit reference

4010.message: Device disconnected during restore (RestoreOS device removed)
board_level_checks: Check USB-C DFU port — on Apple Silicon MacBooks, DFU uses the back-left USB-C port (ATC0)
Check CD3217 port controller for DFU port (UF400 on M1 Air/Pro 13", varies by model)
Check NAND power rails — PP1V2_NAND, PP1V8_NAND on storage pages
Check SSD communication — SoC may hang mid-write causing USB timeout
Check PP5V_S2 stability during restore (monitor with oscilloscope for dips/dropouts)
Try alternate USB-C port if available (not all ports support DFU)
4010.common_repair: Failing SSD causing SoC hang that looks like USB disconnect to host. If USB cable/port swaps don't fix it, NAND subsystem is the likely culprit.
4010.shortcut: soc_force_dfu — if DFU entry itself fails, the problem is USB-C or power, not NAND

4013.message: Device panicked or lost communication mid-restore
board_level_checks: Check PP5V_S2 rail stability with oscilloscope during restore — voltage dips cause ANS2 panic
Check NAND power rails on storage pages (varies by board)
Check NAND chip positions — after reball/replacement, chips MUST be in same positions
On Apple Silicon: ANS2 (Apple NAND Storage controller) panic = one or more NAND chips failing
Check all NAND chips — firmware treats them as single array, one failure requires all replaced
Monitor PP5V_S2 enable signal (P5VS2_PWR_EN from PMU GPIO) for intermittent dropout
4013.common_repair: Unstable PP5V_S2 from UC300 causing power dropout during firmware write. Also: bad NAND chip requiring full NAND set replacement from donor board of same model.
4013.rossmann_case: A2179 (820-01958): 20V 0.06A, SSD failure confirmed, NAND swap required from same model

board_level_checks: Same checks as Error 4013 — these are diagnostically equivalent
Additionally check NAND chip positioning if board was previously repaired
If SoC-level NAND controller (ANS2) is damaged, NAND replacement alone won't fix it
4014.common_repair: Interchangeable with 4013 in diagnosis. NAND failure or PP5V_S2 instability.

4005.message: SSD not detected or battery/power failure during restore
board_level_checks: Verify battery is connected and holding charge — Mac laptops NEED battery for restore
Check NAND detection — if SoC can't see storage, Error 4005
Check NAND power rails on storage schematic pages
Check battery connector and BMU (J5100 gasket on M1 Air, J5150 soldered on M1 Pro 13")
Check SYS_DETECT signal — without battery detection, restore may fail
4005.common_repair: Battery not providing sufficient current during restore, or NAND completely dead.

4041.message: 10-minute timeout waiting for BootedOS state transition
board_level_checks: Check PP1V2_NAND power rail for shorts — one shorted NAND chip drags entire rail
Check for liquid damage indicators near NAND chips
Monitor power cycling pattern: 5V → 19V → 6V cycling = power delivery instability
Check L5800 area for heat generation during power cycling
If one NAND chip is shorted on power rail, ALL NAND chips must be replaced together
4041.common_repair: Water damage causing NAND power rail short. Requires full NAND set replacement.
4041.rossmann_case: A2442 (820-02098): Water damage, UN500 NAND shorted on PP1V2_NAND1, required all 5 NAND chips replaced

9.message: Device disconnected or restore timed out — kernel failed to load ASR
board_level_checks: Check NAND communication and power rails
Check SoC CPU power domains (PCPU from MPMU BUCK0, ECPU from MPMU BUCK11 on M1)
Check SPI NOR flash — if SoC can't read firmware, restore fails
Verify SoC power rails are stable during restore (not just present but STABLE)
9.common_repair: NAND issue or SoC power instability. If NAND checks out, check CPU VCore stability.

14.message: Firmware file verification failure during write
board_level_checks: Try different USB-C cable first — data corruption during transfer causes checksum failure
Check NAND flash for corruption (especially if device was previously low on storage)
Check USB-C connector pins for bent/damaged contacts
If cable swap doesn't fix: NAND subsystem failure
14.common_repair: Start with cable swap. If persistent, NAND flash corruption or failure.

75.message: Missing data volume on APFS formatted device
board_level_checks: Check PP5V_S2 rail stability — voltage drops during writes corrupt APFS metadata
Check PMU bypass capacitors for shorts or opens (cap banks C81xx/C82xx area)
Check NAND power rail filtering — noisy power corrupts flash writes
Multiple DFU restore attempts may clear if software-related; if persistent = hardware
75.common_repair: APFS metadata corruption from voltage instability. Check PP5V_S2 and PMU output cap integrity.

6.message: Could not write firmware partition (BridgeOS on T2, iBoot on Apple Silicon)
board_level_checks: SSD failure — service partition cannot be written
On T2 Macs: BridgeOS partition write failure = storage dying
Check NAND power rails and communication lines
Rule out software interference (third-party security tools)
6.common_repair: SSD failure requiring NAND replacement from donor board.

21.message: Hardware failure — device stuck at recovery/Apple logo
board_level_checks: Verify battery is connected and functional
Check SPMU (U7700) and MPMU (U8100) outputs — run full PMU diagnostic steps
Check PMU_ACTIVE_READY — if low, PMU isn't completing startup
Check 32.768kHz crystal — PMU needs this clock
21.common_repair: PMU failure or dead battery. Connect known-good battery first, then diagnose PMU if still failing.

53.message: Touch ID sensor pairing mismatch with Secure Enclave
board_level_checks: Touch ID sensor is cryptographically paired to logic board — replacement breaks pairing
Check Touch ID flex connector for water damage or corrosion
Check Touch ID sensor continuity
On Macs: Touch ID in power button, paired to Secure Enclave in SoC
53.common_repair: Non-original Touch ID sensor, or water damage to Touch ID flex. AST2 required for re-pairing.
53.note: In Apple Configurator context, Error 53 can mean communication timeout instead — different from iPhone meaning

56.message: NFC chip or Touch ID verification failure
board_level_checks: Check NFC controller IC
Check Touch ID sensor communication
Less relevant on MacBooks (no NFC) — more common on iPhones
56.common_repair: Dead or glitched NFC chip. Primarily an iPhone error.

-1.message: Connection lost — trackpad firmware write failure (Macs) or baseband failure (phones)
board_level_checks: On Macs: trackpad unable to have firmware written during restore
Check trackpad flex cable and connector
Try replacing trackpad — if error clears, trackpad was the issue
Check trackpad power rail and I2C communication
-1.common_repair: Replace trackpad or check trackpad cable. Board-level fault if trackpad swap doesn't fix.

1.message: Firmware version mismatch or downgrade attempt
board_level_checks: Usually software — wrong IPSW file or attempted downgrade
If persistent with correct firmware: possible SoC identification issue (rare)
1.common_repair: Download correct IPSW for the specific model. Not typically a hardware issue.

board_level_checks: Run full diagnostic tree from Step 1
This is a catch-all hardware error — no single subsystem identified
Check all power rails sequentially

40.message: Dead NAND chip or power supply problem
board_level_checks: Dead NAND chip — primary hardware cause
Check NAND power rails
Check for baseband / power supply issues (secondary)
40.common_repair: Dead NAND requiring logic board diagnosis and potential NAND replacement.

board_level_checks: 2001: macOS kernel extension issue — restart host Mac
2002: Connection blocked by another program
2003/2006: Bad USB cable — replace cable, disconnect other USB devices
2005: USB hardware failure on host or target
2009: Third-party USB interference — reset SMC
If persistent after cable/port swap: check CD3217 port controller on target board
2001_to_2009.common_repair: Cable swap first, then port controller diagnosis.

4000.message: Device state machine confusion — not in expected DFU/Recovery state
board_level_checks: Usually procedural — wrong DFU entry sequence
If persistent: SoC or USB controller damage preventing proper state transitions
Verify correct DFU entry procedure for specific model
4000.common_repair: Retry DFU entry with correct procedure. If persistent, SoC-level issue.

PPT004.check: Battery connector, BMU IC, battery flex

PPT005.check: Battery connector seating, BMU gasket (J5100 on Air, J5150 on Pro 13")

PPR001.check: SoC power domains, check PCPU/ECPU rails from PMU

PPM001.check: LPDDR power rails from PMU, check DDR connection to SoC

PPM002_to_015.check: LPDDR power rails, memory connection to SoC — on Apple Silicon, memory is in SoC package

PFM001_to_007.check: On Apple Silicon, SMC is part of SoC. Check MPMU outputs, SoC power domains.

PFR001.check: SPI NOR flash, Secure Element. Try DFU restore first.

PPF001.check: Fan connector, PP5V_S2_TO_FAN power, fan control IC

PPF003.check: Fan connector, power rail, thermal sensor

VDH002.check: NAND power rails, NAND chips, SSD communication

VDH004.check: NAND power rails, check for dying NAND chips

VDH005.check: APFS metadata corruption — check PP5V_S2 stability, try DFU restore

VFD001_to_005.check: Display connector, eDP data lines, backlight driver, display power sequencer

VFD006.check: GPU power domain from PMU (SIMETRA BUCK6 on M1), SoC graphics power pages

VFF001_to_003.check: Audio amplifiers, audio codec (San Diego PMU on M2+), headphone jack, speaker connectors

NDK001.check: Keyboard connector, keyboard backlight controller, keyboard flex

NDR001.check: Trackpad connector, trackpad flex, I2C communication

BMT001.check: Touch ID sensor, Secure Enclave pairing, Touch ID flex

BMT003_to_005.check: Touch ID sensor/connector, AST2 re-pairing may be needed

DFR001.check: Touch Bar connector (JT700/JT710), DFR enable signal, Touch Bar power rails. A2338 M1-specific.

CNW001_to_006.check: WiFi/BT module (Rasputin on M1, varies by model), antenna connections, WiFi power rail

CNW007_008.check: Software reset first — may clear without hardware repair

NDT001_to_006.check: CD3217 port controllers, CIO redrivers (on Pro/Max models), USB-C connector

NDD001.check: USB-C port controller, USB2 repeater, level shifters

NDC001_to_006.check: Camera flex connector, ISP power domain from SoC, camera module

VDC001_to_007.check: SD card controller (GL9755 on Pro models), SD card connector, SD power rail. Pro/Max only.

NNN001.check: SPI NOR flash (serial stored here), Secure Element, SoC. Critical — board may need Apple provisioning.

apple_silicon.amber_sos_morse: Firmware recovery / DFU mode. Board needs restore via Apple Configurator.
apple_silicon.solid_white: Mac is powered on or sleeping
apple_silicon.flashing_white_on_boot: Hardware issue detected — run Apple Diagnostics
apple_silicon.no_led_no_response: Fatal hardware failure — SoC or power delivery. Start diagnostic tree from Step 1.
apple_silicon.exclamation_mark_screen: Firmware needs restoration — use Apple Configurator 2 via DFU

intel_t2.exclamation_mark_boot: BridgeOS corruption — restore T2 firmware via DFU

pre_t2_intel.1_beep: No RAM detected — check memory connection
pre_t2_intel.3_beeps_repeating: RAM failed integrity check — replace memory
pre_t2_intel.3_long_3_short_3_long: EFI ROM corruption — automatic recovery mode
pre_t2_intel.1_long_tone_power_hold: EFI ROM update in progress (pre-2012 models)

dfu_port_by_model.description: Apple Silicon MacBooks require a SPECIFIC USB-C port for DFU restore. Using the wrong port will not work.

ports.A2442_M1_Pro_14: Left rear USB-C (ATC0, closest to display hinge)
ports.A2485_M1_Pro_16: Left rear USB-C (ATC0, closest to display hinge)
ports.note: Host Mac must run Apple Configurator 2. Connect USB-C cable THEN enter DFU (hold power + specific key combo for 10 seconds, release power, keep holding for 10 more seconds).

diagnostic_tree_step_reference.1: PPBUS_AON — Charger path, battery, USB-C/MagSafe input
diagnostic_tree_step_reference.2: PP3V8_AON — SVR AON (U5700), forced start bypass applicable here
diagnostic_tree_step_reference.6: PP5V_S2 / PP3V3_S2 — Standalone VRs (critical for NAND-related errors)
diagnostic_tree_step_reference.6.5: Viper/Austringer VR — Pro/Max only, PCPU/ECPU domains
diagnostic_tree_step_reference.6.6: Monaco/CLVR VR — Pro/Max only, GPU/Fabric domains
diagnostic_tree_step_reference.8: Peripheral subsystems — Display, USB-C, Keyboard, Audio, WiFi, Storage, Trackpad, TouchID, HDMI, SD Card, Touch Bar

quick_triage.description: When you have an error code, use this to jump directly to the right diagnostic step instead of starting from Step 1

nand_errors.note: Most DFU errors (70%+) trace back to NAND/SSD subsystem. Always check PP5V_S2 stability first — voltage dips during writes cause the majority of restore failures.

power_errors.note: These indicate fundamental power delivery failure. Run full tree.

usb_errors.start_at: Step 8 (USB-C peripherals) — check DFU port specifically
usb_errors.note: Start with cable swap. If persistent, check CD3217 port controller on DFU port.

peripheral_errors.start_at: Step 8 (Peripherals) — specific subsystem per error code
peripheral_errors.note: These rarely prevent boot. They block restore completion but board may be otherwise functional.
